authorities.name: California Department of Fish and Wildlife
Lahontan Regional Water Quality Control Board

sources.title: CCR T14 §5.85(a)(2) — Trout, General Statewide Regulations (inland streams, rivers, and canals)
sources.quotedText: All inland streams, rivers, and canals, except those listed in Sections 7.40(b) and 7.50(b), are open to fishing from the last Saturday in April through November 15 with a five-trout daily bag limit and ten-trout possession limit, with no gear restrictions. From November 16 through the Friday preceding the last Saturday in April, a zero-trout bag limit applies, and only artificial lures with barbless hooks may be used for all species of fish. In waters where the bag limit for trout is zero, trout must be released unharmed and should not be removed from the water.

From November 16 through the Friday preceding the last Saturday in April, a zero-trout bag limit applies, and only artificial lures with barbless hooks may be used for all species of fish. In waters where the bag limit for trout is zero, trout must be released unharmed and should not be removed from the water.

sources.title: California Fish and Game Code §7145 — Sport Fishing License Requirement
sources.quotedText: Except as otherwise provided in this article, every person 16 years of age or older who takes any fish, reptile, or amphibian for any purpose other than profit shall first obtain a valid license for that purpose...

sources.title: Truckee River Sediment TMDL — Lahontan Regional Water Quality Control Board (Gray and Bronco Creek watersheds)
sources.quotedText: Gray and Bronco creek watersheds are adjacent drainages located in the eastern portion of the Truckee River TMDL project area, near the California/Nevada state line. They discharge into the Truckee River upstream of the US Geological Survey's (USGS) gauging station at Farad in Nevada County. Both creeks have their headwaters in the Mount Rose wilderness area of Nevada, and the majority of their drainage areas are in Nevada.

sources.title: Notice of Exemption — Truckee River Wildlife Area, Gray Creek Canyon Unit (CEQAnet #2007118221)
sources.quotedText: Truckee River Wildlife Area, Gray Creek Canyon Unit. Donation of 3 parcels of land, totaling 1,343+/- acres, to the Dept. of Fish and Game by the Truckee Donner Land Trust, in order to preserve open space and to protect riparian and wildlife habitats.

sources.title: Preservation group acquires Grey Creek Canyon; Efforts under way for Truckee River land buy — SierraSun.com
sources.quotedText: On a hike this summer through Grey Creek Canyon, Truckee Donner Land Trust Executive Director Perry Norris saw a bear, an eagle and other wildlife common to the Sierra Nevada. "It's really quite stunning," he said of the steep canyon area. Today, months later, the land trust is expected to acquire 135 acres of the canyon using money from Nevada County's Dryden Wilson Bequest Fund.

parameters.note: Gray Creek (Nevada Co.) is a Truckee River tributary near the CA/NV state line, headwatered in the Mount Rose Wilderness and discharging into the Truckee River upstream of the USGS Farad gauge. It is not listed in CCR §7.40(b) or §7.50(b), so it falls under the general statewide regulation for inland streams, rivers, and canals in §5.85(a)(2).

regulations.humanSummary: Open with no gear restrictions from the last Saturday in April through November 15; open catch-and-release for trout (artificial lures with barbless hooks only) from November 16 through the Friday preceding the last Saturday in April (general statewide stream regulation; not listed as a special-regulation water)
regulations.verbatimText: All inland streams, rivers, and canals, except those listed in Sections 7.40(b) and 7.50(b), are open to fishing from the last Saturday in April through November 15 with a five-trout daily bag limit and ten-trout possession limit, with no gear restrictions. From November 16 through the Friday preceding the last Saturday in April, a zero-trout bag limit applies, and only artificial lures with barbless hooks may be used for all species of fish. In waters where the bag limit for trout is zero, trout must be released unharmed and should not be removed from the water.
